coredata.dc:title: Regulating the interconnected microporous structure of porous carbon by alkali metal ions for improved surface and space utilization for organic supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Linna
Li, Yutong
Zhang, Jun
Gan, Xuemeng
Li, Qiqi
Tang, Tingting
Chen, Yaoyao
Zhu, Jiayao
Guo, Nannan
Wang, Luxiang
Zhang, Su
coredata.dc:description: Developing porous carbon with suitable microporous structure and large effective surface area is crucial for pursuing high volumetric performance of organic supercapacitors. Herein, we proposed a homogeneous activation method to regulate the interconnected microporous structure of porous carbon through choosing the alkali metal ion activators (Li+, K+, Cs+). We have revealed the pore evolution mechanism from the points of ion activation strength, cluster transport, and metal boiling points. Compared to others, the optimized sample activated by Cs+ shows large micropore volume, high effective surface area (2463 m2 g−1), and high compaction density (0.75 g cm−3), performing an improved volumetric capacitance (126 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) and good rate performance (79 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1) in organic electrolyte. The symmetric supercapacitor at the commercial electrode mass loading shows a competitive energy density (23.9 Wh L−1 at 211.4 W L−1, 4 Wh L−1 at 1638 W L−1). Moreover, this pore-regulating strategy has a general availability when using other non-foaming carbon precursors, which will open up new paths for improving the surface and space utilization of porous carbon for compact capacitive energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous carbon
Supercapacitor
Alkali metal ion
Volumetric capacitance
Pore regulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Porous structure characterization of PC-Li, PC-K, and PC-Cs: (a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, (b) pore size distributions, (c) pore volumes and compaction densities. (d) Comparison of the specific surface areas and packing densities of the samples with other porous carbon materials. (e) Total surface area (S total) and effective surface area (S D > 0.67 nm, based on the ion size in organic electrolyte) of the samples.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra, (c) carbon yields of PC-Li, PC-K, and PC-Cs. (d) The alkaline metal contents of the samples before and after homogeneous activation without washing measured by ICP-MS.
Schematic diagram of the porous structure evolution of the PC-M.

Electrochemical performance of PC-Li, PC-K, PC-Cs, and commercial activated carbon (YP-50F) in organic supercapacitors: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s−1, (b) gravimetric and (c) volumetric capacitances at different current densities from 1 to 20 A g−1. (d) Gravimetric and volumetric capacitances of PC-Cs compared with the reported porous carbon materials. (e) Nyquist plots and (f) the calculated R
e and R
ef . (g) Comparison of Ragone plots between PC-Cs//PC-Cs with other symmetric supercapacitors at the commercial mass loading. (h) Long-term cycle performance at 10 A g−1.

Porous structure and electrochemical performance of the chitosan-based carbons (CPC-M): (a) Pore size distributions, (b) effective surface area and compaction densities, and (c) CV curves at 10 mV s−1. (d) Gravimetric capacitance and volumetric capacitance at different current densities from 1 to 20 A g−1 of CPC-Cs.

head.ce:title: Regulating the interconnected microporous structure of porous carbon by alkali metal ions for improved surface and space utilization for organic supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830017, Xinjiang, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Chemical Engineering and Energy Materials Research Center, School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Chemical Engineering and Energy Materials Research Center
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Chemical Engineering and Energy Materials Research Center, School of Materials Science and Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors with equal contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interconnected microporous structure of porous carbon has been regulated through alkali metal ion activators (Li+, K+, Cs+), enabling the optimized sample with high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances as well as robust kinetics for organic supercapacitors.

Developing porous carbon with suitable microporous structure and large effective surface area is crucial for pursuing high volumetric performance of organic supercapacitors. Herein, we proposed a homogeneous activation method to regulate the interconnected microporous structure of porous carbon through choosing the alkali metal ion activators (Li+, K+, Cs+). We have revealed the pore evolution mechanism from the points of ion activation strength, cluster transport, and metal boiling points. Compared to others, the optimized sample activated by Cs+ shows large micropore volume, high effective surface area (2463 m2 g−1), and high compaction density (0.75 g cm−3), performing an improved volumetric capacitance (126 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) and good rate performance (79 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1) in organic electrolyte. The symmetric supercapacitor at the commercial electrode mass loading shows a competitive energy density (23.9 Wh L−1 at 211.4 W L−1, 4 Wh L−1 at 1638 W L−1). Moreover, this pore-regulating strategy has a general availability when using other non-foaming carbon precursors, which will open up new paths for improving the surface and space utilization of porous carbon for compact capacitive energy storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The key to solve the above problem is to enlarge the effective surface area and reduce the excessive macro/meso-pores of porous carbons, which can be realized by a rational pore engineering. Traditional porous carbons are usually prepared by physical or chemical activation through an outside-to-inside pore-creating process. The porous structure can be tailored by adjusting the activator dosage, thermal treatment temperature, or activation time [15–17]. However, the non-uniform etching usually creates hierarchically deep and tortuous porous structure with amount of meso- and macro-pores [18,19], resulting in a low compaction density and an insufficient inner space utilization. By contrast, the homogeneous activation method (also called ionic activation) opens up another pathway for pore regulation [20–22]. In this method, kinds of metal ions are uniformly adsorbed/coordinated inside the functionalized carbon precursors [23,24], serving as ionic activators during thermal treatment. The obtained pores have a unique interconnected structure, having the advantage of robust mass transport kinetics. The early studies of homogeneous activation are done by direct pyrolysis of various metal organic salts, such as potassium citrate and potassium gluconate, usually leads to the formation of hierarchically porous carbon due to the severe decomposition, gas release, and foaming during thermal treatment. These porous carbons showed high gravimetric capacitance, but un-satisfactory volumetric performance for supercapacitors due to their low density [25,26]. Recently, through choosing the suitable carbon precursors to avoid severe foaming, the obtained carbons with uniform and interconnected micropore-dominated structure can integrate large surface area and high compaction density, showing improved volumetric performance [27,28]. For example, we have prepared an ultramicroporous carbon through homogeneous activation using oxygenated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and K+ as the carbon precursor and activator, respectively. It showed a high C v value (270 F cm−3) and good rate performance (43% capacitance retention at 100 A g−1) in aqueous KOH electrolyte [29]. However, by summarizing the relative works (Table S1), we realized that the high-density porous carbons prepared by homogeneous activation usually showed a small microporous structure (less than 0.67 nm), which is hard to accommodate and transport large-sized organic ions. Previously, Zhuo et al. proposed a strategy for fine-tuning the porous structure by choosing the suitable alkali metal ions from Li+ to Cs+. The Cs+ activated porous carbon showed high gravimetric and volumetric capacitances in ionic liquid electrolyte [30]. Yuan and Chen et al. also confirmed the differences in activation strength of alkali metal ions. The Ru+ activated porous carbon showed suitable porosity and improved Zn-ion storage capability both in gravimetric and volumetric performance [31]. This inspires us to improve the surface and space utilizations of porous carbons in organic electrolytes by exploring different cationic activators to regulate the microporous structure.

Herein, we use the highly crystallized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as exemplified carbon precursors due to their good thermal and chemical stability (without foaming during thermal treatment). Three kinds of porous carbons were prepared through homogeneous activation using Li+, K+, and Cs+ as the activators in the same GQD mass to ion mole ratio. By changing from Li+ to Cs+, the large-micropore volume and effective surface area of the samples are both significantly increased. We have revealed the pore evolution mechanism from the points of enhanced ion activation strength, easy cluster transport, and decreased metal boiling points. The optimized sample activated by Cs+ shows an interconnected microporous structure with both high surface area (3127 m2 g−1) and compaction density (0.75 g cm−3), performing an improved volumetric capacitance (126 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1) in organic electrolyte and a competitive power density (23.9 Wh L−1 at 211.4 W L−1) in symmetric supercapacitors at a commercial mass loading. Lastly, the general availability of this pore regulation strategy was verified by using the non-foaming chitosan precursor.

The GQDs were prepared through a chemical oxidation method using bituminous coal (Heishan, Xinjiang, China) as the carbon precursor and HNO3/H2SO4 mixing acid as the oxidant. The details are available in our previous studies [32,33].

In a typical process, 0.5 g GQDs were dispersed in 20 mL ethanol to form solution A. The alkali metal hydroxides with a same mole amount (0.18 g for LiOH·H2O, 0.25 g for KOH, and 0.75 g for CsOH·H2O) were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol to form solution B. After uniform mixing the solution A and B through sonication and vigorous stirring for 12 h, the obtained mixtures were dried in an oven at 80 °C. The dried powders were milled and compressed into tablets with a diameter of 10 mm under the pressure of 10 MPa and kept for 10 min. The obtained tablets were put into a N2-filled tubular furnace, heated up to 250 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 for ensuring the sample densification by slowing down the gas release rate [22,29,33]. Then, the temperature was raised up to 800 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, and held for 1 h. After the products were taken out from the N2-filled furnace tube, ca. 5 mL deionized water was quickly added to the sample to react with the highly reactive products (eg. elementary alkaline metals and M2O). The product was washed with diluted HCl solution (1 mol L−1) to remove the inorganic components, and further washed to neutral using deionized water. The final samples were denoted as PC-M, which M represents the alkali metal cations (Li+, K+, and Cs+). Commercial activated carbon (Kuraray YP-50F) was purchased from Shenzhen Kejing Star Technology Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China).

To verify the general availability of the pore regulation strategy, chitosan (deacetylation degree ≥ 95%, M w ≈ 70,000) was also used as the carbon precursor because of its non-foaming feature during thermal treatment [34]. The preparation procedure of the chitosan-derived porous carbon was the same as that of the GQD-derived samples, except that chitosan was dissolved in 5 wt.% acetic acid solution. The obtained samples were denoted as CPC-M, where M also represents the alkali metal cations (Li+, K+, and Cs+).

The morphology and structure of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL-2100F). The crystal structure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). Raman spectra were measured on a Bruker SENTERRA dispersive Raman spectrometer with a wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using an ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer. The surface area was obtained from N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (ASAP 2460, Micromeritics). The pore size distribution was calculated by Non-Local Density Functional Theory (NLDFT) method. The surface area was obtained by Braunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The alkali metal content was tested by the inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Thermo Scientific ICAP RQ). The samples after homogeneous activation were quickly taken out from the N2-filled furnace and weighed. After adding ca. 5 mL deionized water, the ICP-MS measurement was carried out by using the total solid sample and solution.

For preparing the working electrodes, the sample, carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binder were mixed with a mass ratio of 85:10:5 in ethanol to prepare coating slurry, then coated on the current collector of stainless steel mesh, and dried at 110 °C for 10 h under vacuum. Two-electrode symmetric supercapacitors were assembled in a CR2032 battery type. The average mass loading of the active materials was controlled as 2 mg cm−2 when investigating the performance of electrode material. For evaluating the performance under practical condition of symmetric supercapacitor, the average mass loading of each electrode was set as the commercial-level loading of 10 mg cm−2. The organic electrolyte was 1 mol L−1 tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TEABF4) solution in propylene carbonate. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge measurements were conducted on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd., China) with the voltage window of 0–2.5 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz by means of applying an alternating current amplitude of 5 mV. The gravimetric capacitance (Cg , F g−1) of the electrode materials was calculated from the charge–discharge curves according to the equation:
(1)
C g
= I Δ t / m Δ V
where I is the discharge current (A), Δt is the discharge time (s), m is the active material mass on a single electrode (g), and ΔV is the voltage window (V).

The volumetric capacitance (Cv, F cm−3) was evaluated according to the following equations:
(2)
C v
=
C g
× ρ
(3)
ρ = m / V
where ρ (g cm−3) is the compaction density of active material, Cg (F g−1) is the gravimetric capacitance, m (g) and V (cm3) are the mass and the volume of active material. We calculated the volume of active material by measuring the radius (r) and thickness (h) of the compressed active material under the pressure of 10 MPa in a cylinder mold (V = h × π × r 2).

The energy density (E, Wh L−1) and power density (P, W L−1) were calculated by the following equations:
(4)
E = C
( Δ V )
2
/ 8 × 3.6
(5)
P = 3600 E / Δ t
where C is the gravimetric/volumetric capacitance of the symmetric supercapacitor calculated based on the total mass of the two electrodes, ΔV is the voltage window (V) excluding the IR drop, and Δt is the discharge time (s).

The GQDs have an average diameter of 3.2 nm and abundant oxygen-bearing groups at the edge sites. The preparation method and structural details are provided in our previous works [32,33]. Previously, we have characterized the mixture of GQDs and KOH with a same mass ratio as we used in this work by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and XPS. The results demonstrated that the alkali metal ions are uniformly absorbed/coordinated on the GQDs mainly by –COOM form (M represents to the cations) [22]. In the subsequent thermal treatment, homogeneous pore creating by the uniformly distributed alkali metal ions leads to the formation of interconnected porous structure in carbon frameworks [22,35]. SEM images show that all the samples have a similar micron-scale block morphology with the size of ca. 10 μm. The surface of these carbon blocks is smooth, indicating the dense structure derived from homogeneous activation (Fig. S1).

As seen from Fig. 1 a, all the samples show type I N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms consisting of a rapid adsorption in the low pressure region (P/P 0 < 0.1) and a very small hysteresis loop in the middle pressure region (0.5 < P/P 0 < 0.9), indicating the similar micropore-dominated structure with small amount of mesopores. The adsorbed volumes are gradually enlarged when changing the activators from Li+ to Cs+, demonstrating that the total surface area (S total) and pore volume (V total) are significantly increased from PC-Li (810 m2 g−1, 0.42 cm3 g−1) to PC-K (1730 m2 g−1, 0.69 cm3 g−1), and to PC-Cs (3127 m2 g−1, 1.40 cm3 g−1). Importantly, the micropore sizes are gradually enlarged from PC-Li to PC-Cs (Fig. 1b). PC-Li mainly shows an ultramicroporous structure concentrated at 0.58 nm. As a contrast, PC-Cs contains amount of large micropores covering the region from 0.70 to 2.00 nm. Moreover, the mesopore volume of PC-Cs is also sharply increased to 0.51 cm3 g−1, which is more than 5 times higher than those of PC-Li and PC-K (Fig. 1c and Table S2). The significantly enlarged surface area and micropore size confirm the gradually enhanced activation strength from Li+ to Cs+ [30,31]. Homogeneous activation mainly creates uniform micropores in dense carbon scaffold thanks to the non-foaming nature of the GQD precursor. Although the packing density shows a slight decrease along with the increase of total pore volume, PC-Cs with extremely large surface area of 3127 m2 g−1 still maintains a high compaction density of 0.75 g cm−3, superior to commercial porous carbon (YP-50F, 1600 m2 g−1, 0.5 g cm−3) and most of the reported porous carbons (Fig. 1d) [20,36–44].

As the desolvated TEA+ (0.67 nm) has a larger diameter than BF4 − (0.48 nm) in TEABF4/propylene electrolyte, herein, the surface area contributed by the pores larger than 0.67 nm is defined as the effective surface area (S D > 0.67 nm) [45,46]. PC-Cs exhibits the largest S D > 0.67 nm of 2463 m2 g−1 (Fig. 1e), which is 4 times higher than PC-K (675 m2 g−1) and even 1 order of magnitude higher than the ultramicroporous PC-Li (158 m2 g−1). Moreover, the S D > 0.67 nm of PC-Cs accounts for 79% of its S total, also much higher than those of PC-Li and PC-K, indicating the effectiveness of pore regulation by Cs+ for providing effective energy storage sites for organic supercapacitors.

From the thermodynamic view, the rising of activation strength Li+ < K+ < Cs+ has been verified by the negatively changed Gibbs free energy (ΔG) when reacted with carbon [30,31]. Therefore, under the same GQD mass to ion mole ratio, stronger ion activator not only enlarges the surface area and pore size, but also makes a more disordered structure of the samples because of the enhanced etching effect. As seen from the XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a), two representative diffraction peaks located at ca. 23° and 43° of PC-Li are correspond to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon, respectively. For PC-K and PC-Cs, the (002) peaks are weakened and shifted to smaller angles, and the (100) peaks are almost disappeared, indicating the obviously decreased crystallinity. The highly disordered crystal structure of the samples is also seen from the HRTEM images (Fig. S2). The peak area ratio of D and G bands (AD/AG) in Raman spectra represents the defect density of carbon materials [47]. The samples show increased AD/AG values from 1.79 to 2.27, confirming the increased defect density from PC-Li to PC-Cs (Figs. 2b and S3). As a result, PC-Cs shows a decline in the electronic conductivity (64 S m−1) compared to PC-Li (97 S m−1) and PC-K (76 S m−1, Fig. S4). Considering the similar chemical compositions of PC-M from the XPS spectra (ca. 88 at.% C, 11 at.% O, and 1 at.% N, Fig. S5 and Table S3), the decreased conductivity is because formation of porous and disordered structure by Cs+ etching breaks some of the electron transport paths. Nevertheless, PC-Cs still holds a higher electrical conductivity than that of the commercial activated carbon (YP-50F, 39 S m−1) benefiting from the highly conjugated cores of GQD precursor, which would ensure good electrochemical kinetics and rate performance [48,49]. In addition, the decreased carbon yield from 32.4 to 24.8 wt.% further illustrates the lifting etching ability from Li+ to Cs+ (Fig. 2c). Therefore, it seems simple to understand the phenomenon of surface area enlargement and pore size expansion by changing the ions from Li+ to Cs+. However, PC-K and PC-Cs still have certain amount of ultramicropores with the size of ca. 0.5 nm, which are smaller than those created by Li+ (0.58 nm, Fig. 1b). To explain this “unusual” phenomenon, the underlying mechanism of homogeneous pore creation by different ion activators should be considered in detail.

As the alkali-metal-bearing intermediates (elementary substance, M2CO3, or M2O) formed during homogeneous activation are highly reactive and corrosive, it is hard for us to identify their structure and composition because they will quickly change when exposing in air, or corrode the in-situ measurement equipment. Fortunately, the carbon activation by alkali metal hydroxides [50,51], and the functional group evolution during thermal treatment have been studied a lot [52,53], giving us support and inspiration to deduce the pore evolution mechanism. As shown in Fig. 3 , the homogeneous activation process can be divided into three steps. First, the alkali metal ions are firmly adsorbed and/or coordinated on GQDs mainly by –COOM form (Reaction (6) [22]. Then the –COOM groups undergo decomposing at 200–600 °C, forming ultrafine M2CO3 and M2O clusters due to the strong anchoring effect and confinement of dense carbon scaffold (Reaction (7) and (8). As the main activation agents, these clusters are evenly distributed in the carbon frameworks, which is beneficial for creating interconnected micropores. When the temperature continues increasing to 600–800 °C, the GQDs are chemically welded together to form continuous carbon scaffold [29]. The vigorous carbon etching and pore creation take place via Reaction (9), simultaneously forming alkali metals through carbon thermal reduction.
(6)
GQD - C O O H +
M
+
+
OH
-
→ G Q D - C O O M +
H
2
O
(7)
- C O O M →
M 2
C
O 3
+ C O
( 200 -
400 °
C )
(8)
M
2
C
O
3
→
M
2
O + C
O
2
( 400 -
600
°
C )
(9)
M 2
O + C → C O + 2 M
( 600 -
800 °
C )

According to the results, we have deduced the pore evolution mechanism. The greatly improved activation strength from Li+ to Cs+ has been proved by thermodynamics and the previous works [30,31]. During the homogeneous activation process, since Li2O has an extremely high melting point of 1567 °C, the generated Li-bearing clusters are difficult to migrate below 800 °C. Thus, they prefer local concentration and etching, leading to the formation of narrowly distributed 0.58 nm pores (Fig. 3). By contrast, the melting points of K2O and Cs2O are lower than 500 °C, making them easy to transport in the pores of the carbon scaffold. Therefore, when K2O and Cs2O clusters are formed, part of them go through carbon etching to form metal element, leaving behind ultramicropores with the size of ca. 0.5 nm. Meanwhile, the ultrafine clusters tend to in-pore migrate and aggregate to form bigger ones for reducing the systematic energy [54]. Subsequent etching lead to the creation of interconnected micropores with enlarged pore size. Besides, considering the rank of boiling points Li (1347 °C) > K (774 °C) > Cs (678 °C) and the reaction equilibrium, evaporation and removal of the metal product also promote carbon etching and pore creating (Reaction (9). The ICP-MS results of the un-washed samples show more obviously decreased contents of K and Cs compared with Li (Fig. 2d). Thus, the promoted Reaction (9) is another reason for the strong etching of the carbon scaffold. We can see that Cs has the highest activation strength and the lowest boiling point, enabling PC-Cs to integrate large surface area, large micropore size, and high compaction density.

We first measured the capacitive performance of the samples by assembling symmetric supercapacitors using TEABF4/ propylene carbonate electrolyte with the electrode mass loading of 2 mg cm−2. By measuring the samples prepared at different carbonization temperatures, we find that 800 °C is the optimized temperature for achieving good capacitance and rate performance (Fig. S6). In addition, by measuring the samples prepared using different amount of alkali metal activators, we find that the PC-K and PC-Cs under the GQD mass to ion mole ratio of 1 g to 0.0089 mol show the optimized volumetric performance (Fig. S7 and S8). The CV curves (Fig. 4 a) and galvanostatic charge–discharge curves (Fig. S9) show that all the samples present rectangular and triangular shapes at a voltage window of 0–2.5 V, respectively, indicating that their storage mechanism are mainly the electric double-layer behavior [8]. In this case, the gravimetric capacity is positively related to the effective surface area. As PC-Cs holds the largest effective surface area, it exhibits the highest capacitances of 168, 156, 141, 127, 116, and 106 F g−1 at 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, and 20 A g−1, respectively, which are much better than those of PC-K and commercial activated carbon (YP-50F). In comparison, PC-Li fails to store large-sized organic ions with the capacitance less than 20 F g−1 due to its ultramicroporous structure (Figs. 4b and S10). Importantly, owing to the high compaction density, PC-Cs shows high volumetric capacitances of 126 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 and 79 F cm−3 at 20 A g−1 (Fig. 4c), superior to commercial porous carbons (80–110 F g−1, 50–70 F cm−3) and most of the reported porous carbon materials (Fig. 4d) [36,55–61].

To reveal the underlying mechanism of the improved electrochemical performance, electrochemical kinetics of the samples are characterized by EIS analysis (Fig. 4e and f). The intersections to the real axis are almost overlapped, suggesting the similar electric resistances (R
e ) of the assembled devices. As there is no charge-transfer process (electrochemical reactions) for an electrical double-layer capacitor, the semicircle diameters in high frequency region are ascribed to the impedance of electrical double-layer formation process, defining as R
ef in this work [59]. An obvious decrease of the semicircle diameters demonstrates the enhanced electric double-layer formation kinetics from PC-Li to PC-K, and to PC-Cs. PC-Cs presents the smallest R
ef of only 4.4 Ω, which is almost 6.3 times lower than PC-Li (28.0 Ω) and 2.6 times lower than PC-K (11.5 Ω). Moreover, the tails in the low frequency region of PC-Cs is more vertical than the others, indicating the fast in-pore ion migration kinetics due to the enlarged micropore size. Therefore, the integration of good gravimetric, volumetric, and rate performance of PC-Cs is mainly ascribed to the well-regulated microporous structure by Cs+ activator, enabling large effective surface area, high compaction density, as well as interconnected and efficient ion migration channels.

To evaluate the energy storage performance under practical condition, we have assembled the PC-Cs//PC-Cs symmetric supercapacitor with a commercial-level loading of 10 mg cm−2 for each electrode. The PC-Cs has good electric conductivity, interconnected porous structure, and suitable micropore size, which can provide sufficient electron and ion transport paths even at high mass loadings. At this mass loading, PC-Cs shows slightly decreased capacitance (162 F g−1 /122 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1 according to the galvanostatic charge–discharge test), but still much better than the YP-50F activated carbon (Fig. S11 and S12). As seen in Fig. 4g, the PC-Cs//PC-Cs supercapacitor exhibits a considerable volumetric energy density of 23.9 Wh L−1 at the power density of 211.4 W L−1, which is much higher than that of the YP-50F//YP-50F supercapacitor, and comparable to other reported organic supercapacitors at the similar electrode mass loadings [59,62,63]. Moreover, the device shows good cycle stability with a 97% capacitance retention after 30,000 charge–discharge cycles at 10 A g−1, maintaining the Coulombic efficiency of 100% during cycling (Fig. 4h). Such good electrochemical performance proves that elaborate regulation of the interconnected microporous structure of carbon is important for integrating high gravimetric, volumetric, and rate performance of carbon materials, satisfying the miniaturization and lightweight development of supercapacitors.

As high-cost and tedious-preparation of the GQDs severely restrict the application of PC-Cs, to prove the general availability of this pore-regulating strategy via alkali metal ions, we choose the low-cost and natural-abundant chitosan as the carbon precursor due to its non-foaming feature during thermal treatment [64,65]. Similar as the GQD-derived porous carbons, the surface area and pore size of the chitosan-based carbons (CPC-M) also present a gradual enlargement with the increase of activation strength from Li+ to Cs+. Owing to the highest pore volume and enlarged micropore size (Fig. 5 a and Table S4), CPC-Cs has the largest effective surface area of 2936 m2 g−1 and an ultrahigh compaction density of 0.80 g cm−3, even better than PC-Cs (Figs. 5b and S13). As a result, CPC-Cs performs superior gravimetric and volumetric capacitances, achieving 154 F g−1 and 123 F cm−3 at the current density of 1 A g−1 (Figs. 5d and S14). In addition, we also conducted similar experiments with other carbon precursors. We found that when using the precursors with foaming behavior (such as starch), the porous structure can also regulate to a certain extent. But the obtained porous carbons show relatively low compaction densities because severe gas release during pyrolysis results in large volume expansion and un-controllable formation of meso- and macro-pores. These experimental results can also be verified by previous reports [66–69]. Therefore, preparing dense and porous carbon by this pore-regulation strategy is more applicable to the carbon precursors with good thermal stability and non-foaming feature during pyrolysis. But how to screen the non-foaming carbon precursor is still under studying. It is considered that the foaming phenomenon is relevant to the pyrolysis and activation kinetics of carbon precursors, which will also be the focus of our further research.

In summary, we have demonstrated a pore-regulating strategy by different alkali metal ions (Li+, K+, and Cs+) to tailor the microporous structure of porous carbons for compact energy storage in organic supercapacitors. The GQD-derived porous carbon activated by Cs+ exhibits an expanded micropore size, a large effective storage surface area (S D > 0.67 nm, 2463 m2 g−1), and a high compaction density (0.75 g cm−3), which offers a boosted surface and space utilization for accommodating and transporting large-sized organic ions. The optimized sample performs high volumetric capacitance of 126 F cm−3 at 1 A g−1, superior to the commercial YP-50F activated carbon and comparable to other reported porous carbons in organic supercapacitors. The assembled symmetric supercapacitor at commercial electrode mass loading of 10 mg cm−2 shows high volumetric energy density of 23.9 Wh L−1 at 211.4 W L−1. The pore regulation mechanism is deduced as the enhanced ion activation strength, easy cluster transport, and decreased metal boiling point from Li+ to Cs+. Furthermore, homogeneous activation can significantly reduce the amount of alkaline activator (about 6 times). We have also proved the general availability of this strategy for preparing dense and porous carbon by selecting the carbon precursor with non-foaming characteristic during pyrolysis. The strategy may open up a new way in designing dense carbons for high-volumetric-performance energy storage.

Linna Zhu: Writing – original draft. Yutong Li: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Methodology. Jun Zhang: Methodology. Xuemeng Gan: Formal analysis, Methodology. Qiqi Li: Formal analysis. Tingting Tang: Methodology. Yaoyao Chen: Formal analysis. Jiayao Zhu: Methodology. Nannan Guo: Formal analysis, Supervision. Luxiang Wang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Su Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52062046 and 22268040), the Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region, China (2020D01C019), the Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation, China (No. ZR2022QE063).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.158051.

ce:section.ce:para:
